beats.text: A scoring classifier commits to no decision until you pick a threshold, and sweeping it traces every operating point the model can offer.

visual.sub: Sweep the threshold, trace the menu

beats.text: The ROC curve plots true positive rate against false positive rate, and both rates condition on the true class.

visual.note: Both rates condition on the true class, so ROC is invariant to prevalence.

beats.text: Because both axes condition on the true class, the ROC curve is invariant to class prevalence and bows toward the top-left as separation grows.
The area under the curve equals the probability that a random positive outranks a random negative, with ties split evenly.
AUC of one is a perfect ranker, half is a coin flip, and below half means the score is backwards.
The worked example forms six positive-negative pairs, finds five concordant with no ties, and gives AUC five over six.

visual.label: AUC from 5 of 6 pairs ranked correctly

beats.text: ROC's prevalence-invariance is a feature when judging a ranker in the abstract, and a trap the moment you deploy it.

visual.sub: The story ROC hides on rare events

beats.text: On a one-percent fraud problem a model can post a gorgeous AUC and still raise fifty false alarms per real fraud.

visual.label: AUC that still drowns in false alarms

beats.text: Precision carries false positives in its denominator, so it is acutely sensitive to prevalence in a way the ROC rates are not.

visual.note: The no-skill baseline is the positive rate, not a fixed diagonal.

beats.text: As positives get rarer the PR curve collapses toward the shrinking baseline while AUC barely moves at all.
The practical rule: use ROC to compare rankers, use precision-recall when positives are rare and false alarms cost real money.
Flagging fifty cases with thirty truly positive gives precision of zero point six, a number ROC never puts in front of you.
Credit risk has its own ranking dialect, and two numbers dominate banking model documentation: the KS statistic and the Gini coefficient.

visual.sub: Discrimination in coordinates a risk committee reads

beats.text: KS is the largest gap between the cumulative score distributions of bads and goods, the maximum vertical distance from ROC to the diagonal.

visual.note: KS ranges from 0, identical curves, to 1, perfectly disjoint.

beats.text: In retail credit a KS in the thirties or forties is a healthy application scorecard, while above seventy-five usually means a leak.
Gini is just AUC rescaled so a random model scores zero and a perfect model scores one.
Gini is a fixed function of AUC, but KS depends on the shape of separation and can reorder two models AUC ranks the other way.
PSI shares the distribution-comparison spirit but answers a different question: not discrimination, but whether the population has shifted.
Every ranking metric judges ordering and ignores the actual value of the score, but a decision usually needs the number to mean something.

visual.sub: When the score has to mean something

beats.text: Perfect calibration means that among all cases assigned probability p, a fraction p actually turn out positive.

visual.note: Calibration and discrimination are orthogonal properties.

beats.text: A reliability curve plots mean prediction against observed frequency, and a curve sagging below the diagonal signals an over-confident model.
The Brier score is the mean squared error of the probabilities themselves, a strictly proper rule you cannot game by shading forecasts.

visual.note: Lower is better; predicting the base rate gives pi times one minus pi.

beats.text: When a model ranks well but is miscalibrated you recalibrate with Platt scaling or isotonic regression, both of which preserve the ranking exactly.
Two true-positive predictions of zero point eight and zero point nine give a Brier score of zero point zero two five.
The ROC curve hands you every operating point, but it does not tell you which one to stand on.

visual.sub: The default 0.5 is almost always wrong

beats.text: The right threshold minimizes expected cost, a weighted sum over the confusion matrix that depends on numbers the model never sees.

visual.note: Choose the threshold t that minimizes this total.

beats.text: Sweeping the threshold traces a U-shaped cost curve, and the minimum sits well away from the naive midpoint.
For a calibrated probability the cost-optimal cutoff has a clean closed form depending only on the ratio of error costs.

visual.note: Equal costs give 0.5, the only case the default is right.

beats.text: If a missed case costs nine times a false alarm, the optimal cutoff slides down to zero point one.

visual.label: cost-optimal threshold when FN costs 9x FP

beats.text: The formula is only valid on calibrated scores, which is exactly why calibration comes before cutoff selection in the pipeline.
